skill.name: game-art
skill.description: Game art principles. Visual style selection, asset pipeline, animation workflow.
skill.summary: A guide to game art development covering style selection, 2D and 3D pipelines, color theory, animation principles, and asset organization.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer flagged 10 potential issues including external_commands and weak cryptographic algorithms. After manual review, all findings are FALSE POSITIVES. The backtick characters detected are markdown formatting syntax, not shell execution. The cryptographic alerts appear to be scanner bugs triggered by random words. This is a purely informational markdown documentation file with no executable code, network calls, or file system operations.

high_findings.title: External Commands False Positive
high_findings.description: Scanner detected backtick characters in markdown as shell execution. These are actually markdown code formatting syntax (lines 19, 36, 149, 156, 160 contain ASCII diagrams and code examples). No code execution present.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Backtick characters in markdown are formatting syntax, not executable code. The file contains only documentation about game art principles.

high_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm False Positive
high_findings.description: Scanner flagged random words as cryptographic algorithms. No cryptographic code exists in this file - it is purely a game art documentation guide.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines contain words like 'description', 'Visual', 'Appeal', 'Desktop' - no cryptographic algorithms exist in this documentation file.

content.value_statement: This skill provides comprehensive game art principles including visual style selection, asset pipeline workflows, color theory, and animation guidelines to help developers create polished game visuals.
actual_capabilities: Guide visual style selection based on game mood and budget
Explain color theory applications for games
Detail animation principles with frame count guidelines
Suggest resolution and scale standards by platform
Define asset naming conventions and folder structures
limitations: Does not generate actual art assets or files
No tool-specific tutorials for software like Blender or Photoshop
Does not provide code for implementing art in game engines

use_cases.title: Choosing Art Style for Indie Game
use_cases.description: A solo developer needs to decide on an art style for their first game. The skill provides a decision tree comparing pixel art, vector, hand-painted, and other styles with production speed and hiring considerations.
use_cases.target_user: Indie game developers and small teams

use_cases.title: Setting Up Asset Pipeline
use_cases.description: A team starting a new project needs to establish their art production workflow. The skill outlines phases for 2D and 3D pipelines from concept to engine integration.
use_cases.target_user: Game development teams and technical artists

use_cases.title: Learning Game Animation Basics
use_cases.description: A programmer turned game developer wants to understand animation principles for better communication with artists. The skill covers the 12 animation principles applied to games.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Style Consultation
prompt_templates.prompt: I am making a [type] game with [budget] budget. Help me choose an art style.
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs quick art style recommendations

prompt_templates.title: 2D Pipeline Planning
prompt_templates.prompt: What tools and workflow should I use for a 2D game sprite pipeline?

prompt_templates.title: Animation Frame Planning
prompt_templates.prompt: How many frames should I use for a [action] animation in a [type] game?

prompt_templates.title: Platform Resolution Guide
prompt_templates.prompt: What resolution and sprite sizes should I use for a [platform] game?

output_examples.input: I am making a retro platformer with a small budget. What art style should I choose?
output_examples.output: For a retro platformer with a small budget, Pixel Art is ideal. It offers nostalgic appeal, manageable production complexity, and works well for solo developers. The skill provides a decision tree to confirm this choice and suggests keeping the palette limited for cohesive visuals.

output_examples.input: How should I organize my game asset files?
output_examples.output: Use the naming convention [type]_[object]_[variant]_[state].[ext] such as spr_player_idle_01.png. Organize folders by: characters/, environment/, ui/, effects/, and audio/. This ensures easy navigation and prevents naming conflicts.

output_examples.input: What are the 12 animation principles applied to games?
output_examples.output: The key principles include: Squash & Stretch for impacts, Anticipation for wind-up movements, Staging for clear silhouettes, Follow-through for dynamic elements, Slow in/out for easing, Arcs for natural paths, Secondary Action for breathing/blinking, Timing for weight/speed, and Exaggeration for readability.

best_practices: Define a clear style guide before production begins
Choose resolution and work at source, never scale down
Test art at actual gameplay distance for readability
anti_patterns: Mixing random art styles without consistency

faq.question: What is the best art style for a solo developer?
faq.answer: Pixel art or vector/flat styles are best for solo developers due to lower skill barriers and faster production times.

faq.question: How do I choose between 2D and 3D for my game?
faq.answer: Consider your team skills, budget, and target platform. 2D has lower production costs while 3D offers more immersion but requires more resources.

faq.question: What resolution should I use for pixel art games?
faq.answer: Common resolutions range from 320x180 to 640x360, with sprite sizes of 16-32 pixels for characters.

faq.question: How many frames does a walk cycle need?
faq.answer: A typical walk cycle uses 6-12 frames for smooth movement. Slower games can use fewer frames while faster-paced games benefit from more.

faq.question: Can this skill create art assets for me?
faq.answer: No, this skill provides guidance and principles. It does not generate actual images, animations, or 3D models.

faq.question: What tools does the skill recommend for 2D art?
faq.answer: Common tools include Aseprite for pixel art, Photoshop or Krita for illustration, and Spine for 2D animation.
